title: What is MySQL's window function PERCENT_RANK?
options: PERCENT_RANK() = (rank - 1) / (total_rows - 1); returns relative rank as 0 to 1 value. First row is always 0. Like RANK() but normalized. CUME_DIST() is similar but = count of rows <= current / total
correct_answer: PERCENT_RANK() = (rank - 1) / (total_rows - 1); returns relative rank as 0 to 1 value. First row is always 0. Like RANK() but normalized. CUME_DIST() is similar but = count of rows <= current / total

title: Write a query to implement a search ranking system.
description: Create a search with relevance scoring.
correct_answer:
SELECT id, title, description,
  (CASE WHEN title LIKE CONCAT('%', @search, '%') THEN 10 ELSE 0 END +
   CASE WHEN description LIKE CONCAT('%', @search, '%') THEN 5 ELSE 0 END +
   CASE WHEN title LIKE CONCAT(@search, '%') THEN 15 ELSE 0 END
  ) AS relevance_score
FROM articles
HAVING relevance_score > 0
ORDER BY relevance_score DESC, created_at DESC
LIMIT 20;

title: What is MySQL's CHECK constraint?
description: How do CHECK constraints work in MySQL 8.0?
options: CHECK(condition) enforces data validation at the database level; e.g., CHECK(age >= 0 AND age < 200), CHECK(status IN ('active','inactive')). MySQL 8.0.16+ actually enforces them (earlier versions parsed but ignored them)
correct_answer: CHECK(condition) enforces data validation at the database level; e.g., CHECK(age >= 0 AND age < 200), CHECK(status IN ('active','inactive')). MySQL 8.0.16+ actually enforces them (earlier versions parsed but ignored them)

title: Write a query using LATERAL join for correlated subqueries.
description: Use LATERAL to write efficient correlated queries.
correct_answer:
-- Get top 3 orders per customer (MySQL 8.0.14+)
SELECT c.id, c.name, o.order_id, o.total
FROM customers c,
LATERAL (
  SELECT order_id, total
  FROM orders
  WHERE customer_id = c.id
  ORDER BY total DESC
  LIMIT 3
) o
ORDER BY c.id, o.total DESC;

title: What is MySQL's generated (computed) column?
options: ALTER TABLE t ADD full_name VARCHAR(200) GENERATED ALWAYS AS (CONCAT(first_name,' ',last_name)) STORED/VIRTUAL; VIRTUAL computed on read, STORED persisted on disk (indexable). Useful for computed fields, JSON extraction, derived data
correct_answer: ALTER TABLE t ADD full_name VARCHAR(200) GENERATED ALWAYS AS (CONCAT(first_name,' ',last_name)) STORED/VIRTUAL; VIRTUAL computed on read, STORED persisted on disk (indexable). Useful for computed fields, JSON extraction, derived data

title: Write a query to implement a tagging system with efficient filtering.
description: Query items by multiple tags efficiently.
correct_answer:
-- Find articles tagged with ALL specified tags
SELECT a.id, a.title
FROM articles a
JOIN article_tags at ON a.id = at.article_id
JOIN tags t ON at.tag_id = t.id
WHERE t.name IN ('python', 'backend', 'api')
GROUP BY a.id, a.title
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT t.name) = 3  -- must match all 3 tags
ORDER BY a.created_at DESC;

title: What is the difference between pessimistic and optimistic locking in MySQL?
options: Pessimistic: SELECT...FOR UPDATE locks rows; prevents conflicts upfront; best for high contention. Optimistic: uses a version/timestamp column; check version on UPDATE; retry on conflict; best for low contention, better throughput
correct_answer: Pessimistic: SELECT...FOR UPDATE locks rows; prevents conflicts upfront; best for high contention. Optimistic: uses a version/timestamp column; check version on UPDATE; retry on conflict; best for low contention, better throughput

title: Write a query to calculate session duration from event logs.
description: Compute user session lengths from raw events.
correct_answer:
WITH sessions AS (
  SELECT user_id, event_time,
    CASE WHEN TIMESTAMPDIFF(MINUTE, LAG(event_time) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time), event_time) > 30
         OR LAG(event_time) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time) IS NULL
    THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS new_session
  FROM user_events
),
numbered AS (
  SELECT *, SUM(new_session) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time) AS session_id
  FROM sessions
)
SELECT user_id, session_id,
  MIN(event_time) AS session_start,
  MAX(event_time) AS session_end,
  TIMESTAMPDIFF(SECOND, MIN(event_time), MAX(event_time)) AS duration_seconds
FROM numbered
GROUP BY user_id, session_id;

title: What is MySQL's functional index?
description: How do functional indexes work in MySQL 8.0?
options: CREATE INDEX idx ON t ((LOWER(email))); indexes the result of an expression, not just a column. Supports JSON extraction: ((CAST(data->>'$.age' AS UNSIGNED))). Eliminates need for generated columns just for indexing
correct_answer: CREATE INDEX idx ON t ((LOWER(email))); indexes the result of an expression, not just a column. Supports JSON extraction: ((CAST(data->>'$.age' AS UNSIGNED))). Eliminates need for generated columns just for indexing

title: Write a query to find patterns in time-series data.
description: Detect spikes and anomalies in metrics.
correct_answer:
WITH metrics AS (
  SELECT recorded_at, value,
    AVG(value) OVER (ORDER BY recorded_at ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg,
    STDDEV(value) OVER (ORDER BY recorded_at ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_std
  FROM sensor_data
  WHERE recorded_at >= DATE_SUB(NOW(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
)
SELECT recorded_at, value, moving_avg, moving_std,
  CASE
    WHEN value > moving_avg + 2 * moving_std THEN 'SPIKE'
    WHEN value < moving_avg - 2 * moving_std THEN 'DROP'
    ELSE 'NORMAL'
  END AS anomaly_type
FROM metrics
WHERE value > moving_avg + 2 * moving_std
   OR value < moving_avg - 2 * moving_std
ORDER BY recorded_at;
